person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
William of Douglas (died c.1214) was a medieval nobleman living in Clydesdale, an area under the control of the King of the Scots.

«b»Enigmatic origins«/b»
The origins of William are uncertain, the first of the name of Douglas to appear on historic record. He appears as witness to a charter of Jocelin, Bishop of Glasgow in 1174 in favour of the monks of Kelso Abbey, at which time he was in possession of the Lands of Douglas.

«b»Sholto/William«/b»
David Hume of Godscroft in his history refers to the progenitor of the House of Douglas, Sholto. Gleaned from the works of Buchanan and Boece, Godscroft's narrative explains that during the reign of a King Solvathius, Sholto Douglas was instrumental in putting down an uprising by a usurper Donald Bain in 767AD, and as reward was granted the lands that would after be called Douglas.

Both Balfour Paul and Maxwell agree that this origin tale is mythic, but do contest that William of Douglas was active at the time of the real rebellion of the Meic Uilleim, under their chief Domnall mac Uilleim. The earlier historians may have confused the mythic Donald Bain with Domnall Bán mac Domnaill, the penultimate Meic Uilleim chief.

This may be corroborated by the facts that the lands of Douglas marched with those of the leader of King William I of Scotland's retaliatory forces, Lochlann, Lord of Galloway. William may well have been a vassal of the Lord of Galloway. Furthermore, all of William's sons with the exception of the eldest were to hold privileged ecclesiastic positions within the former Meic Uilleim territories in Moray.

«b»Issue«/b»
William of Douglas may have married Margaret, a sister of Freskin of Kerdal, a Flemish laird from Moray. He had issue:

1.) Archibald I (1166-1213), Lord of Douglas, married Margaret, daughter of Sir John Crawford of Crawfordjohn

2.) Bricius de Douglas (ca. 1195-1222), Bishop of Moray

3.) Alexander de Douglas (ca. 1195-1238), a canon of Spynie, vicar capitular of Elgin

4.) Henry de Douglas (ca. 1195-1245), a canon of Spynie

5.) Hugh de Douglas (ca. 1171-1245), a canon of Spynie, Archdeacon of Moray

6.) Freskin de Douglas (1205-September 1232), parson of Douglas, later Dean of Moray

7.) Margaret de Douglas (1177-1260), married Hervey de Keith, Marischal of Scotland

zh.lifeSummary: 當William le Fleming Douglas I Laird Of Douglas於1150年出生在蘇格蘭米德洛迪安苗斯頓時，他的父親Sir Theobald le Fleming30歲，母親Kerdale De Moravia18歲。他於1172年之前與Margaret Kersdale of Moray在蘇格蘭結婚。他們至少是5個兒子和1個女兒的父母。他去世於1214年在蘇格蘭藍納可爾道格拉斯享年64歲，埋葬在英國蘇格蘭斯可迪夏 波德斯凱爾索。

nameMeanings.name: Douglas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish: habitational name from any of various places called from their situation on a river named with Gaelic dubh ‘dark, black’ + glas ‘stream’ (a derivative of glas ‘blue’). There are several localities in Scotland and Ireland so named, but the one from which the surname is derived in most if not all cases is Douglas in Lanarkshire 20 miles south of Glasgow, the original stronghold of the influential Douglas family and their retainers.

History: The family taking their name from Douglas in Lanarkshire were of Flemish origin. They rose to great prominence in the 14th and 15th centuries, controlling the earldoms of Douglas, Morton, and Angus, and later, Queensberry.

variants.name: Douglass
meanings: Scottish and English (Durham and Northumbria): variant of   Douglas  .

History: William Douglass, a physician recognized for his identification and description of an epidemic of scarlet fever, was born c. 1691 in Gifford, Haddington County, Scotland, and settled in Boston in 1718. The abolitionist, orator, and journalist Frederick Douglass assumed the name after escaping from slavery in 1838 and traveling to Massachusetts. Son of a white father and a slave with some Indian blood, he was born Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey c. 1817 in Tuckahoe, MD.

nameMeanings.name: Laird
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: status name for a landlord or landowner, from northern Middle English laverd ‘lord’.

variants.name: Lard
meanings: Scottish: altered form of   Laird  .

variants.name: Leard
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Laird  .

nameMeanings.name: Of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sung, Jung, Yong, Chang, Kwang, Sang, Dong, Hyun, Jae, Jin, Kyung, Jong, Chong, Chung, Byung, Jeong, Pyong, Myong, Hak, Jin Hwan, Sook, Yeong, Choon.
Korean: variant of   O  .
Japanese: variant of Ō (see O ).

variants.name: O
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Bo Young, Chae, Chong Hwan, Dong Kun, Ho, Hwan, Hyun, Jong, Jung, Ki Soon, Kwang.  Korean Min, Chong, Chol, Jung Ho, Myong, Sa, Sung Kyun.

Korean (오; Chinese character: 吳): historically, there were two Chinese characters for the O surname, but by the late Koryŏ period (14th century AD ), one of them had disappeared. Some records indicate that there are as many as 210 different clans for the remaining character, but only sixteen can be positively documented. All sixteen of these clans originate from the same ancestor, O Ch’ŏm (吳 瞻), who migrated to Korea from China during the reign of Shilla's King Chijŭng (500–514 AD ). O is a common surname throughout Korea. Compare Oh .

nameMeanings.name: Douglas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish: habitational name from any of various places called from their situation on a river named with Gaelic dubh ‘dark, black’ + glas ‘stream’ (a derivative of glas ‘blue’). There are several localities in Scotland and Ireland so named, but the one from which the surname is derived in most if not all cases is Douglas in Lanarkshire 20 miles south of Glasgow, the original stronghold of the influential Douglas family and their retainers.

History: The family taking their name from Douglas in Lanarkshire were of Flemish origin. They rose to great prominence in the 14th and 15th centuries, controlling the earldoms of Douglas, Morton, and Angus, and later, Queensberry.

variants.name: Douglass
meanings: Scottish and English (Durham and Northumbria): variant of   Douglas  .

History: William Douglass, a physician recognized for his identification and description of an epidemic of scarlet fever, was born c. 1691 in Gifford, Haddington County, Scotland, and settled in Boston in 1718. The abolitionist, orator, and journalist Frederick Douglass assumed the name after escaping from slavery in 1838 and traveling to Massachusetts. Son of a white father and a slave with some Indian blood, he was born Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey c. 1817 in Tuckahoe, MD.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

nameMeanings.name: le
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: 1  ♂ ‘Profit’ or ‘sharp’.
2  ♀, ♂ ‘Upright’.

nameMeanings.name: Fleming
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: ethnic name for someone from Flanders, from Middle English fleming. The word reflects a Norman French form of Old French flamanc ‘Fleming’, from the stem flam- + the ancient Germanic suffix -ing. In the Middle Ages there was considerable commercial intercourse between England and the Netherlands, particularly in the wool trade, and many Flemish weavers and dyers settled in England. This surname is also common in south and east Scotland and in Ireland, where it is sometimes found in the Gaelicized form Pléamonn.
German: variant of   Flemming  , cognate with 1 above.

variants.name: Flemings
meanings: English: plural form of   Fleming  .
Americanized form of Flemish Vleminckx: patronymic from Vleminck, an ethnic name for someone from Flanders, Middle Dutch vleminc.

variants.name: Flanders
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, an altered form of Flanders, which originally denoted someone who came to England from that region. Its occasional use as a given name is probably in honour of the explorer Matthew Flinders ( 1774–1814 ). Flinders Petrie ( 1853–1942 ) was a famous British archaeologist.

variants.name: Fleeman
meanings: English: variant of   Fleming  .

variants.name: Flemming
meanings: German: ethnic name for someone from Flanders, Middle High German vlaeminc. People from Flanders spread throughout Germany, as well as England, in the Middle Ages as clothmakers and dyers.
English: variant of   Fleming  .

variants.name: Flamenco
meanings: Spanish: ethnic name from flamenco ‘Fleming, Flemish person’ (see Fleming ).

en.lifeSummary: When William le Fleming Douglas I Laird Of Douglas was born in 1150, in Hermiston, Midlothian, Scotland, his father, Sir Theobald le Fleming, was 30 and his mother, Kerdale De Moravia, was 18. He married Margaret Kersdale of Moray before 1172, in Scotland. They were the parents of at least 5 sons and 1 daughter. He died in 1214, in Douglas, Lanarkshire, Scotland, at the age of 64, and was buried in Kelso, Scottish Borders, Scotland, United Kingdom.

Il a épousé Margaret Kersdale of Moray avant 1172, à Écosse. Ils ont eu au moins 5 fils et 1 fille. Il est décédé en 1214, à Douglas, Lanarkshire, Écosse, à l’âge de 64 ans, et a été enterré à Kelso, Scottish Borders, Écosse, Royaume-Uni.

ko.lifeSummary: William le Fleming Douglas I Laird Of Douglas(이)가 1150년 스컷랜드 미들로스이언 허미스턴에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Theobald le Fleming(은)는 30세였고, 그의 어머니 Kerdale De Moravia(은)는 18세였다. 그는 1172년 이전 스컷랜드에서 Margaret Kersdale of Moray(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 다섯 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1214년 스컷랜드 랜아르크실르 더글라스에서 64세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 스코틀랜드 스콧이시 볼더스 켈소에 묻혔다.

Он умер в 1214 г. в местности доуглас, ланаркшир, Шотландия в возрасте 64 лет и был похоронен в местности келсо, скоттиш бордирс, Шотландия, Великобритания.
